planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring; in zone 9 sow September–November for fall-winter harvest or January–February for spring. Speckles is an Austrian heirloom butterhead type with excellent cold hardiness and some heat tolerance. In zone 9 it is most productive from October through March; the red-speckled pattern on the leaves intensifies in cool weather. Successive sowings every 3 weeks extend the harvest season.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained soil; Speckles is an Austrian heirloom butterhead lettuce distinguished by large, soft, pale-green leaves heavily spotted and speckled with red-maroon freckles — a distinctive appearance unlike any other lettuce. The speckle pattern varies from plant to plant, and no two heads are identically spotted, making it one of the most visually interesting lettuces for mixed salads and fresh presentations. The flavor is classic butterhead — tender, buttery, mild, and slightly sweet — without the bitterness of some red-pigmented lettuces. Speckles is a slow-to-bolt Austrian type that holds quality in the garden longer than many butterhead varieties before heat causes it to flower. The unique spotted appearance makes it highly marketable at farmers' markets and popular in mesclun mixes where the speckled leaves stand out visually. In zone 9 it produces excellent fall-through-winter heads with the speckle pattern intensifying in cool temperatures.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Speckles Lettuce Heritage Variety
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Austrian Heirloom Lettuce Types
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Butterhead Lettuce

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring; in zone 9 sow September–November for fall harvest or January–February for spring. Yugoslavian Red is notable for maintaining red coloration even in warm fall conditions when many red lettuces revert to green, making it the most reliably red butterhead for zone 9 fall planting. Successive sowings every 3 weeks through fall extend the harvest window. Harvest whole heads or strip outer leaves for continuous production.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained soil; Yugoslavian Red Butter Lettuce is a large-headed butterhead type from the former Yugoslavia, producing open, loose heads of deeply wine-red outer leaves that contrast dramatically with the pale green-to-yellow interior heart — one of the most visually striking red butterhead lettuce varieties. The large heads weigh 8–12 ounces at maturity with a buttery, tender, mild flavor characteristic of the butterhead type. Yugoslavian Red maintains its deep red color even during the warmer stretches of fall in zone 9, when most red lettuces revert to green with heat — this color stability is a primary reason it is favored for warm-climate fall gardens. The loose, open head structure forms quickly and is ready for harvest at 50–60 days. The deep red outer leaves are high in anthocyanin antioxidants; the green interior provides visual contrast when the head is split or leaves are separated in a salad.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Yugoslavian Red Butter Lettuce Heritage Variety
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Eastern European Heirloom Lettuce
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Red Butterhead Types

planting.note: Direct sow in early spring or fall; in zone 9 sow January–February for spring or September–October for fall. Nevada is particularly tolerant of heat and is one of the best choices for extending the spring lettuce season in zone 9 — it holds without bolting 1–2 weeks longer than most lettuce varieties under warm conditions. Successive sowings every 3 weeks extend the harvest window. Shade cloth in late spring reduces bolting pressure.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained soil; Nevada is a French batavian-type lettuce selected specifically for heat tolerance and slow bolting — it holds usable quality in warm spring conditions 1–2 weeks longer than standard butterhead or looseleaf varieties before flowering, providing a meaningfully extended harvest window in warm climates like zone 9. The large, crisp, slightly frilly green leaves form a dense, semi-heading rosette similar to a looseleaf but with better structure and more crunch than floppy butterhead types. The flavor is fresh, mild, and slightly crisp — closer to iceberg in texture but without the blandness, and closer to looseleaf in flavor but with more body. Nevada is widely used by commercial producers in California and the Southwest who need a heat-tolerant green-leaf lettuce for late spring production windows. In zone 9 it is one of the most reliable lettuces for bridging the February–May spring season before summer heat ends production.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Nevada Lettuce Variety Notes
University of California Cooperative Extension — Heat-Tolerant Lettuce Varieties
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Batavian Lettuce

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or early spring; in zone 9 sow September–November for fall harvest or January–February for spring. Medallion is compact and fast-maturing, making it excellent for succession planting every 2–3 weeks to maintain a continuous supply of small heads. In zone 9 the relatively quick maturity (45–55 days) allows multiple crops in the cool season window. Pull entire plants at harvest.

directions: Direct sow or transplant in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained soil; Medallion is a mini-romaine type lettuce producing compact, upright, 5–6 inch heads with tightly wrapped inner leaves and crisp, crunchy texture — the small-head format that has become standard in grocery produce departments and upscale food service. Mini-romaine heads like Medallion are harvested at one-quarter to one-third the size of full-sized romaine but with the same crunchy, mild, slightly sweet romaine flavor in a single-serving portion size. The compact heads also allow significantly closer spacing (6–8 inches) than full-sized romaine, making them more efficient in raised beds and small gardens. The crisp, upright inner leaves hold dressings exceptionally well for Caesar-style preparations, and the compact whole head can be split lengthwise and grilled or broiled as a dramatic presentation. Medallion has been widely adopted in restaurant supply chains precisely because the single-serving size reduces food waste compared to large head lettuces.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Medallion Mini-Romaine Variety Notes
University of California Cooperative Extension — Mini-Romaine Lettuce Production
Cornell University Vegetable Varieties for Gardeners — Compact Romaine Types

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplant; in zone 9 start February–March and transplant March–April. Pistou can also be direct-sown in warm soil in April. A second planting in July provides fall production. The naturally compact, globe-forming habit needs minimal maintenance — pinch flower buds as they appear to extend the leaf harvest.

directions: Start indoors and transplant into rich, well-drained soil in full sun; Pistou Basil is a French compact dwarf basil named for the Provençal paste pistou — the French equivalent of Italian pesto made with fresh basil, garlic, olive oil, and sometimes cheese and tomatoes. The plant forms a naturally round, globe-shaped mound of small to medium-sized aromatic leaves with a rich, clove-spicy basil flavor slightly sweeter and less anise-forward than Italian sweet basil. The compact habit (8–16 inches, self-branching) makes Pistou ideal for container growing, window boxes, and herb gardens where space is limited. Unlike Fino Verde with its tiny, fingernail-sized leaves, Pistou produces slightly larger leaves that are easier to harvest individually and better-suited to chopping for pistou paste, compound butter, and herb-infused oils. The two cultivars — Pistou and Fino Verde — are distinctly different: Pistou is more upright and globe-forming, Fino Verde is a lower spreading mound with smaller leaves. In zone 9 both provide an excellent warm-season herb harvest.
sources: Territorial Seed Company — Pistou Basil Variety Notes
University of California Cooperative Extension — Culinary Herb Varieties for California
Richters Herb Catalogue — French Dwarf Basil Types
